does: This paper proposes EvoJail, an evolutionary algorithm-based framework for automatically generating diverse jailbreak prompts against large language models. The method formalizes jailbreak generation as a multi-objective black-box optimization problem and uses evolutionary principles including selection, mutation, and recombination to iteratively search for effective prompts. EvoJail employs instruction-fusion techniques to combine jailbreak strategies and evaluates candidates by probing target models directly, selecting and varying prompts based on model responses. The framework aims to generate jailbreaks that generalize across different model versions and exhibit diverse attack patterns rather than converging to narrow, repetitive exploits.
connects: This work extends prior research on automated jailbreak discovery for LLMs, building on earlier methods that search for adversarial prompts through optimization techniques. It responds to limitations in existing automatic jailbreak generation approaches that fail to account for the evolving safety mechanisms in newly fine-tuned model versions and tend to produce homogeneous attacks. The paper connects to broader research on adversarial examples and black-box optimization, applying evolutionary algorithms—a well-established technique in adversarial machine learning—to the specific problem of prompt-based attacks. It also engages with interpretability and red-teaming literature emphasizing the importance of diverse attack vectors for comprehensive safety evaluation.
enables: EvoJail provides a systematic approach to continuously probe safety improvements in LLMs as models evolve, supporting ongoing red-teaming and safety assessment. By generating diverse jailbreak prompts, the framework can help identify blind spots in safety training and reveal different vulnerability classes. This work enables future research on adaptive defenses that anticipate diverse attack strategies and could inform better safety fine-tuning procedures. The evolutionary framework is generalizable to other domains requiring diverse adversarial prompt generation and could be extended to multi-modal systems or more complex attack scenarios. The emphasis on adaptability across model versions suggests avenues for studying the arms race between safety mechanisms and jailbreak techniques.
